sentences.text: All right.
When we were liberated, we walked in-- we walked into-- we walked into a German camp.
The German camps, there were flags.
We took off the flags.
I don't know from where we got the thread.
But they had somebody to cut it.
I made my own blouse.
I was wearing, as a matter of fact, a red blouse and some kind of a skirt.
That's the-- I don't remember where the undergarments what we had.
And with that, I came all the way to-- to Kosice.
That's when I traveled there.
In Kosice-- in Kosice, I met a-- I met a man.
He was going back to where I lived, to [?
Karpaten ?].
I came with him.
When I got out off the train, at first, it's-- it's called [?
Teresow. ?] That was the train is coming.
It was like three miles, three kilometers to go back home that I walked.
I came into town.
When I came into [?
Teresow, ?] people had asked me, are you the one who happened with your sisters?
And I didn't know what they were talking about it.
I came home.
I went to the house, because the house was locked up.
Our neighbors helped themselves to some-- to some land.
They just cut around and left us a little land in the back, because we had a nice land in back of our home.
I went up in the attic, and I found two pictures.
Then I went back to one of-- in town.
There was a house in-- in one court.
There were two homes.
Let me see.
There was [INAUDIBLE].
And we got together there.
And they told me partly what's happened.
They really didn't want to tell me the whole story.
But then somebody came, my cousin's friend, and he told me all about the story what happened to my family.
Yossel.
He's all right.
He's alive.
He's here.
He's in the mountains.
So he told me all about it.
For the second night I was there, my other cousin, Simon-- I have two cousins left here-- Simon Eigelman-- he is here in [?
Boca ?]-- and he says that-- and they said, there's nothing here for you.
You are coming with me.
We are going to Israel.
So we left that morning.
We got across.
That was [INAUDIBLE], Czechoslovakia.
And we went into Sighet.
That was Romania.
Romania, we almost got caught because they wouldn't let-- the Russian would not let us pass by.
And I went in.
I asked-- there I asked for a permit.
How do I get across?
What do you want to do there?
Well, I told them my brother is in the hospital that was the biggest hospital there.
And I want to visit him.
Who are you with?
With two friends.
So he says, I can only let one friend go with you.
And he gave us a pass for two people.
Of course, we went into the bathroom, and from the two, we made that three.
We passed that's all what you have to go what's a bridge.
We went over the bridge, and there was a field, two roads.
So we weren't sure which road to take.
We took one road.
All at once, comes running a Russian soldier-- halt, halt-- with drawing rifle pointing at us.
I says, my goodness, this is the end.
Well, we were lucky.
He says, you took the the wrong road.
We got back to Sighet.
The next morning, my cousin got married.
She met her-- her fiance.
She was engaged before the war, and she met her fiance, and--
Perl Gedalovich.
She is-- she is with us.
She is in New York.
She's still-- she is with us, thank God.
I haven't got too many left.
No.
No, it's her married name.
Her married name is Perl Gedalovich.
Her maiden--
Her maiden name-- hmm-- I think it was [?
Gluck, ?] because she was from a different marriage, so I'm not sure.
I think it was [?
Gluck, ?] Perl [?
Gluck. ?] So my cousin and I start off to go on the way to Israel.
We went-- I don't know if I should tell it or-- we went in a coal train.
We were hidden where they kept the coal.
We paid for them very well to go from one, like go back from Romania to Budapest.
Budapest, I joined a kibbutz, Dror Haganah.
From there, we were supposed to go to Israel.
Well, we were there for a while.
My cousin was-- got engaged.
Simon.
Also got engaged, but--
Simon Eigelman.
But as it happened, he went out something to the market.
And they caught him.
He did not have any papers.
I think must have been the Russian.
They asked for papers, and he didn't have papers.
He couldn't prove who he was.
So they put him to jail.
Being I was there with a group, we-- we decided to leave, because I didn't know what happened to him.
But his fiancee remained.
Then we were going over the mountains overnight till we get to Italy.
It wasn't--
Well, first we went from there to Austria.
By trucks.
There was always by trucks.
There was already working the-- the-- [INAUDIBLE] not the [INAUDIBLE].
What is the organization, the one who helped out the people?
First the Israel, we had then the [INAUDIBLE], which were taking us from the kibbutz.
From the kibbutz, we-- he got stuck halfway, and we wind up in the [INAUDIBLE].
In Austria.
Because they put us up there till we were able to go any further.
The people from the kibbutz put us up because, you know, it wasn't that easy to go from-- [LAUGHS] so I wind up in [INAUDIBLE] with part of the group remained in the city.
Part went up to the hotel.
In the hotel, that's where I met my husband.
He was there with a group of five men.
[SIGHS] You're going to have to ask that my husband.
I don't remember.
[LAUGHTER]
It's in [?
Innsbruck. ?] But I don't remember the hotel.
I don't remember the hotel.
They were just waiting after the war.
They were also just partially-- you know, we didn't go one specific place.
We did not have a place where to go or what to do.
We were just going from place to place to go someplace, because there nothing doing home.
We could not stay home.
We had to go somewhere, so all of us were on the way to Israel.
So that's where I met him.
We were there like for three weeks, I think.
When our trucks ready to move to take us away, take as further, he decided to come with us and take along all his six friends.
Well, when we got to Italy, we were in Cremona and-- in a DP camp.
So, and the truck belonged to who?
To the--
I think must have been to the [INAUDIBLE], because it was part of the kibbutz.
Yes.
When we got to Cremona, my husband found out that he had family in America.
So he got in touch with them, and they send us papers.
So it's a loss for Israel.
So we came to the United States.
But--
But we got married in Italy.
In Italy.
We got married in Cremona, which was 1947, January 2, 1947.
In Cremona.
In the DP camp.
There was a DP camp, yes.
It was called Cremona.
Cremona DP camp.
The camp closed I think that was-- we got married in '47.
I think must have been maybe were '48 or something.
Because '49 we came here.
Then the camp closed.
Must have been or '47-- wait-- or '48, something.
It were '47 or '48, the end of it.
My husband was working at two places there.
I mean, they would give us their provision.
They were giving us out food, the cook.
But whoever was able to cook, they gave us dry provision.
And you should have seen what was doing in back of the building.
Everybody was cooking.
They had those thermoses with the gasoline, and cooking outside our own way of cooking.
To supplement it, my husband had a little fruit store.
And he was also a policeman, which supplemented a little bit, you know, whatever.
We were able to go to a movie, or buy some extra fruit, or buy some extra clothes.
And that's where in Cremona, we had ORT.
It was very tough to get in.
I went into ORT to learn how to sew.
I wanted to learn Italian, so I had a dressmaker.
I went in to work for her just to learn the Italian, the dialect speak.
And I picked it up very fast.
I-- I-- It's easy for me to pick up languages.
And from there, we went-- we took us when the camp closed, we went on to Trana.
Trana.
Trana, T-R-A-N-A, Trana.
And from there, we were there till 1949 before we came to the United States.
United States, we came--
A very religious rabbi.
I don't know the name.
As a matter of fact, it's a shame, because this rabbi had one prayer book.
And whomever he married, he gave us the book to read to get familiar with certain laws.
When I got the book, and there was somebody else's name, [?
Gussman ?] is the name.
He had given it to her when he-- when he married her.
He needed it for me.
He took it away from her.
In fact, years later, when I met her, he said, you know, you still have my prayer book.
I have it.
It's right now it's-- it's an antique.
It's a [NON-ENGLISH], which is a nice, thick [NON-ENGLISH].
It has everything in it.
So I had to go get through all the rituals.
We had to go to the mikveh.
You know what a mikveh is?
You had a mikveh in Cremona?
Yes.
I had to go to the mikveh.
We were supposed to have gotten married on New Year's Eve, but being I didn't let him know enough in advance, I had to wait till the 2nd.
We got married in a little restaurant.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We brought in our own food, our own food.
I mean, we invited quite a big wedding.
I haven't got a picture of it here.
I was staying all night the night before with my friends.
We bought-- we bought fish.
We were cooking fish to take it along to the restaurant, gefilte fish.
And we brought our own-- our own food, whatever we had there, and drinks.
And my husband's best friend we-- I got married in my girlfriend's dress.
She had made a dress for her wedding.
It was made by hand.
And my husband had one suit already at that time.
So we had to lend our suit to his best man because it was a light suit.
And somebody lent him a dark suit to get married.
I mean, that's how rich we were.
But somehow, we lived with hopes for tomorrow.
Then we found out-- my husband found out that his brother is living.
My husband has a brother here.
He almost killed our dog.
We had a little puppy--
In Cremona.
And when he picked up the mail, and he saw from America, and he opened it up that from his brother, he got so excited, he threw the puppy on the floor--
--and ran to see, you know, what's what.
He just couldn't believe it.
And there, I didn't work.
But I did work in Trana.
When I get to Trana, he was, I think, also a policeman.
And I worked in a bath house, which they wanted-- I had some people, which worked with them.
I was there like a guard to make sure that the women go in the women's side, and the men go in the men's side, if something's missing, all that.
So we got some extra supplement.
And from there, we went to [INAUDIBLE], the last camp.
Oh, that's interesting.
[LAUGHS] Before we got married, we lived in a room, which is was, I think, 28, a very big room.
It was mixed people.
Then we had a big hall.
It was being that it was a military camp, we had a very long white hall, a very long hall.
We got some carpenters, and they made this individual little rooms, which was all closed up like with sheets.
So we had our own privacy.
Of course, our own privacy next door was the same thing, just was a sheet between us.
But he made like three or four individual little sheets.
And cooking, like I said, we were cooking in the back of the building.
[LAUGHS] We survived.
From there, to went to [INAUDIBLE].
At that already, I got pregnant.
We were married-- this was '47-- and we were not ready for children.
We were waiting, hoping to get here.
We settle.
But I went to a doctor, and he says, if I would be you, you're married three years, try it.
You might have some problem.
Well, of course, I tried not get pregnant, which we were both hysterical.
We're going to a new country, no money, no place, no idea where do you go?
But the doctor was so nice.
He sat down and talked to me.
He knew me because I went to him as an interpreter with one of my friends.
He was a gynecologist.
He sat down and talked to me.
He says, look.
I can give you an abortion.
But he says, it's your first child.
Don't.
Somehow, you will survive.
That was the happiest time when they put my son on my chest when we came here.
It was tough, because we came here, no profession.
My husband didn't have no profession.
Of course, I didn't have either.
He went in, and he worked in a millinery.
He's going to tell you the rest of the story.
Then finally, something happened.
He went in to learn the butcher business.
From Italy to the States, we came by boat, [?
That was the name of the boat, military boat.
And they were not very nice.
They sold all the food, whatever they did in Italy.
When we, on the way back, there wasn't enough food for us.
That's all what they kept us, with hard-boiled eggs and Rice Crispies and milk.
And they were sitting-- and they were sitting in the captain's room and having all those fancy dishes.
But we were afraid.
You know, when you go back [INAUDIBLE], you're afraid to say anything.
They knew we are not going to report them that we were all hungry.
Just a funny thing, I have to tell you what happened on the boat.
Being I was pregnant, they-- I was in the fifth month.
And they told me, if you stay another two weeks, you can-- they will put you on the plane.
I think I had to be in the six months or so.
But I told them I was fine.
I'm going.
But while I was on the boat, on the ship, rather, I went down to the nurse, and I told her I was pregnant, if I could possibly get a chair.
We didn't get any chairs.
If we wanted to sit, we will have to sit on the floor.
They didn't treat us right, all right?
Let's put it this way.
So she says, you know what?
Here is a folding chair.
Take it up.
The next morning, they had about 40 people pregnant.
[LAUGHTER] That was the funniest thing.
My husband worked, and they took him down.
They had to work on the ship.
They took him down in the room where the engines were, he saw a can of sauerkraut.
He brought up a five pound of can, or whatever.
It was sauerkraut, and opened up, and was giving everybody like a little bit to feed all of us, to make us feel better.
We were sick.
It was-- the-- it was a very rough trip because it was in December.
So it was a very rough trip.
So there was-- people were so sick.
And on the funniest part, [LAUGHS] a wife tells her husband, you wanted to go to America.
We could have gone to Australia.
[LAUGHS] Australia-- it's the joke going around-- would have taken instead of two weeks, whatever it took us, three times as long.
You know, we made little jokes about everything while going along.
Well, we came here, and we lived in the Bronx, a five floor, the fifth floor walk-up.
Yes.
My baby, I had in the Bronx.
By that time, we already lived in Brooklyn.
[LAUGHS] We-- we-- where we lived, the lady was very nice.
I had come back to see her.
I was in the fifth floor.
And she was very nice to us.
And she says, look.
If you don't find an apartment, you are welcome to stay here.
I have a crib in the basement, which was my daughter's, you know, grandchildren.
You can have it.
They were very nice people.
They treated us--
Don't ask me for names, please.
I don't remember.
We were looking for an apartment.
And somehow, somebody found out that they had an empty apartment.
So there was a furnished room.
Jewish people, yeah.
From there, we got an apartment in Brooklyn, which was one of the toughest time to get an apartment in 1949, 1950, because all the people came back from the war, just after the war.
I don't want to go into detail how we got it, but we got an apartment.
We lived there for about-- I don't know-- five years or so.
From there, we moved to the island on Glen Oaks.
We already had two children at that time.
My second son was born there.
Mark.
Mark.
Second one is Edward.
In Hebrew, it's [INAUDIBLE] is my younger one.
And the second one is [INAUDIBLE]-- got to think a bit-- [INAUDIBLE]
Eleazar, ?] I can't think of it right now.
Sorry.
Just too much, what was going on today.
My head is just jumping.
So--
[SIGHS] Well, I'm trying.
It's not easy.
Then we moved to Glen Oaks, the Garden Apartments.
By that time, my husband already was a butcher.
He got a job in Great Neck.
My children went to Great Neck to school.
And when we decided to try for a little girl, which we were very happy to have another little boy.
[LAUGHS]
Robert, and Eleazar is his second name.
And we lived there in Glen Oaks.
I'm trying to rush it.
I don't know how much time we have--
--to get into the tapes.
[SIGHS] Well, I like to tell my children, be good.
Make sure that you are OK with charities.
The main thing, don't forget who you are.
Whatever you do, we love you.
And try to do the best you can.
We love you all.
[SIGHS] This message I'm trying to give you is my memories.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Try to give it to your children, to your grandchildren, and for our future generation.
Never forget the 6 million Jews.
I know you will try.
Because once we are gone, it will be a tough time to let people know there was a very bad war, [? very ?] [? crucial. ?] And we went through from the ashes to life.
And never forget who you are.
Oh--
I don't know.
OK.
Yes, I would like to.
Well, I met my husband in Austria.
We got married in Italy.
And we are married 49 years.
We have three lovely children, and lovely daughters-in-law and grandchildren.
This is part of my life.
Shall I say the other half?
This is part my half.
We went through high and low, hot and cold.
But I'm thankful to God that we met, and to him.
He's very good to me and to the children.
I hope and pray that God lets us stay here for a long, long time yet.
His name is Josef, Josef Schwartz.
Yosek ?] [?
Note ?] Schwartz.
Thank you.
Well.
And we are coming a long way.
Half way around the world.
And we met.
We are happy, and the future looks good.
Thank you.
All right?
Well, I'm the lucky guy.
I got the best woman there is.
We are married for 49 years.
We went through hell together, ups and downs.
And I'm continue to support the whole family.
And the kids are good to us.
And we hope we're going to see our weddings, bar mitzvahs of [INAUDIBLE].
We went through plenty.
We're not dead.
So we are looking future for [INAUDIBLE].
Thank you.
We're having a bar mitzvah coming up for our grandson.
And I'm hoping that God would be good to us, and I can walk down the aisle with my granddaughter, Beth.
The other ones are still young.
So we hope we will be around for a long, long time.
Thank you.
This is a school picture made in Krive of my little sister's school, public school in 1938.
From the right, the second on the right, her name was Gitel Halpert.
On the second row in the middle, the third one, is Sura Miller, my cousin, little cousin, which is same school.
All the way on the top right there is Mendel Berkovich, my cousin.
The other ones are all our friends' children, which I don't remember the names.
This is a picture of my cousin [?
Moishe. ?] He is standing.
I cannot identify the girl.
I think it was taken in Krive, 1939.
This is a picture taken in Cremona in a DP camp.
The bottom left is our friends, Nathan and Rosalyn Sherofski.
The third one, it's myself, Rose Schwartz and Josef Schwartz.
The rest of them are all friends, which we met.
They are all survivors.
We met in Cremona, and we-- we had at the gathering.
This is a picture in Trana.
We had some cousins visiting.
It's 1948, leaving the DP camp, going to-- I don't know the destination.
This is a picture taken [INAUDIBLE], Italy, just before our friend left.
His name was [?
Surlek ?] [?
Shmerkovich. ?] Next to him is Rose Schwartz and Josef Schwartz.
This is a picture taken Margate, New Jersey, of my oldest son and his family.
From the right, it's Mark Schwartz, Beth, Joyce, and Michael Schwartz.
The picture was taken in 1992.
This is a picture from my second-- of my second son.
The top is Dorothy, my daughter-in-law.
From the left is Rebecca and [?
Alora, ?] and Edward Schwartz.
It was taken in 1993.
This is a picture of my youngest son.
From the left is Dana.
In the middle is our granddaughter, Beth, And this is Robert, our youngest.
They're all very precious, those.
The date it was taken in 1992.
This is a picture taken of all the girls in the Cremona Club in 1995 in New York.
